content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


November 5, 2003


Susan M. Schectman, General Counsel
Regional Open Space
330 Distel Circle
Los Altos, CA 94022-1404

Re:    Your Request for Informal Assistance
       Our File No. I-03-233

Dear Ms. Schectman:

       This letter is in response to your request, on behalf of the Midpeninsula Regional
Open Space District, for informal assistance regarding the mass mailing provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because we lack full information on the contents and
format of the Book, and on the Book's actual distribution and sale, we can offer you only
informal assistance.²

QUESTION

       Is the District's soon-to-be-published Trail Guide subject to the Act's restriction
on mass mailings?

CONCLUSION

       Yes. To avoid violation of the Act's mass mailing restriction, it will be necessary
to ensure that the District and the publisher together do not distribute, by means listed in
regulation 18901(a)(1), more than 200 copies of the Book in any single calendar month.


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Informal assistance does not furnish the immunity provided by formal written advice.
(Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. 1-03-233
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Midpeninsula Regional Open Space District (the "District"), headquartered in
Los Altos, is a special district formed under the provisions of Public Resources Code
§ 5500 et seq. The District's purpose is to acquire and preserve open space lands for
public use and enjoyment in San Mateo and Santa Clara Counties. The District's current
jurisdictional boundaries encompass about 330 square miles, where it operates 26 open
space preserves and owns or manages approximately 50,000 acres of open space lands.
Properties acquired by the District are typically maintained in their natural condition for
public recreation and enjoyment of the trails. The District is governed by an elected
seven member board of directors.

In celebration of the 30th anniversary of its founding, the District has undertaken
a project to publish and sell a District Trail Guide ("the Book"), which will be published
and distributed at District expense. Although the District hopes to recoup its costs by
sales, this cannot be guaranteed.

The Book is predominately a trail guide (approximately 75%), with chapters
describing the features of each open space preserve, including key trails/routes, natural
resources (including flora and fauna), geological/ecological features, and cultural history.
The remainder (approximately 25%) tells the District's story, including a brief history on
the formation of the District, the projected future of the District, descriptions of current
public programs, resource management projects, and some staff and volunteer profiles.

Use of Board Member Quotes and Photographs

District staff plan to include District board member quotes and photographs in the
Book. Staff directed the author of the book (an outside consultant hired by the District)
to interview a number of people, including current board members. The board member
quotes that appear in the manuscript were selected and placed at the discretion of the
author, not at the request or direction of board members. In addition, any photographs of
board members (whether current or archival) that are used in the Book are selected at the
discretion of District staff, not the request or direction of board members.

Book Distribution

Initially 6,500 copies of the Book will be produced. The publisher will distribute
5,800 copies, some of which would be sold "over the counter" at bookstores, while others
may be offered for sale over the Internet, at the publisher's own website and/or through
booksellers like Amazon.com. Seven hundred copies will be provided to the District.
From these, 75 copies will be provided to the author, and up to 35 more will be given to
the publisher for promotional activities. The District may sell the remaining 590 copies
at full retail price, at production cost, or it may give them away as gifts. The District will
advertise the Book on its website, in its quarterly newsletter Open Space Views, and at
other special events such as local art and wine festivals. The District itself will not

File No. 1-03-233
Page No. 3

distribute more than 200 copies per month, but cannot forecast the publisher's sales
volume for any given month.

ANALYSIS

Section 89001 provides that "[n]o newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent
at public expense." Regulation 18901 implements § 89001, and provides that a mailing is
prohibited if it meets all of the following criteria:

"(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the
recipient at his or her residence, place of employment or
business, or post office box. For purposes of this
subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered to the recipient must
be a tangible item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or
a written document.
(2) The item sent either:
(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency
which produces or sends the mailing, or
(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other
reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agency
which produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared or
sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert
with the elected officer.
(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with
public moneys; or
(B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding
$50.00 are paid with public moneys, and the design,
production, or printing is done with the intent of sending
the item other than as permitted by this regulation.
(4) More than two hundred substantially similar items are
sent, in a single calendar month, excluding any item sent in
response to an unsolicited request and any item described
in subdivision (b)."

1. Delivery to the end-user.

A communication may be a "mailing" within the meaning of § 89001 only when
it has been "delivered" to the recipient in some tangible form. If the recipient picks up an
item at a counter, bookstore, or similar distribution point, the item is not considered to
have been "mailed" to the recipient. Therefore books distributed by "over the counter"
sales at bookstores or agency facilities, although they are tangible items, are not subject
to the Act's mass mailing restrictions.

You have indicated that the District intends to advertise the Book on its website
and in its newsletter, suggesting that the District may take orders electronically or over

File No. I-03-233
Page No. 4

the telephone, and deliver the books to the purchaser. Books delivered to purchasers at
their home or business addresses will count as "mailings" subject to § 89001.3

We understand that the Book will also be distributed by the publisher, and that at
least some of these books are likely to be sold "over the counter" at bookstores. Books
distributed to the end-user in this manner are not, of course, "mailings." But you indicate
that the publisher may also offer the book for sale over the Internet, through its own
website and/or through booksellers like Amazon.com. Books purchased from such
sources are typically "delivered" to the recipient in a manner that will classify those
books as "mailings" under § 89001.

You should be aware that, even if the District refrains from "mailing" in excess of
200 copies per calendar month, the District might still find itself in violation of § 89001
by reason of the publisher's distribution activities. The Lavagetto Advice Letter, No. A-
90-199, observed that an agency which sent its brochures to public locations for pick up
must take reasonable precautions to prevent redistribution of the brochures by a third
party to the homes or workplaces of more than 200 persons per month. This point has
been reaffirmed in the Anaya Advice Letter, No. A-91-215, the Gladwell Advice Letter,
No. A-95-162, and the Mount Advice Letter, No. A-95-223.

Although we have not previously had occasion to apply this principle to a
commercial publisher's distribution of books produced at public expense, the prohibition
on mass mailings would have little effect if the law could be avoided simply by arranging
for third-party distribution of materials produced at public expense. Accordingly, the
District will be subject to the restrictions of § 89001 whether it distributes the Book itself,
or has agreed to its distribution by its publisher, if more than 200 copies of the Book are
"mailed" by either party, or by both in combination, within any single calendar month.

2. Elected officials featured, named, pictured, quoted and discussed.

An item that is "mailed" within the meaning of § 89001 is not subject to the
statute unless the item either (A) "features" an elected officer affiliated with the District,
or (B) includes the name, office, photograph or other reference to the officer, and is sent
or prepared with the cooperation of that officer.

Regulation 18901(c)(2) specifies that an elected officer is "featured" in a mailing
if the officer's photograph or signature appears therein, or if references to the officer are
highlighted through a variety of formatting devices. We understand that the Book will
contain photographs of and references to at least two elected officers (board members)
affiliated with the District. We understand that photographs of these two officers will

_________________________

3 An order sent in response to information about the Book presented in the District's newsletter
cannot be exempted from mass mailing limits on the ground that it is an "unsolicited request" for a copy
of the book. (See regulation 18901(a)(4), and the definition of "unsolicited request" at regulation
18901(c)(4).)

File No. I-03-233
Page No. 5

appear in the Book, and in that case the two officers will be "featured" in the Book.4
We have not reviewed a copy of the Book, and so cannot determine whether these or
other officers will be "featured" in other ways, but it seems clear from your account of
the facts that at least two of the District's officers will be "featured" in the Book.

Even if an item does not "feature" an elected officer as narrowly defined, the item
is still subject to the restrictions of § 89001 if it otherwise refers to an elected officer and
is produced or distributed "in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the
elected officer." It is our understanding that two or more elected officers will be named
and discussed in the book, and that these officers have cooperated with the author and/or
others working to produce and distribute the book. So long as this is the case, the Book
would be subject to the restrictions of § 89001 even if it does not "feature" an officer
affiliated with the District. (See, generally, Felton Advice Letter, No. A-90-052.)

3. The use of public monies in production or distribution.

It is clear from your account of the facts that public monies in excess of $50 will
be used for design, production, and printing of the Book. We presume from your account
of the facts that more than $50 of District funds – public monies – will be spent in the
design, production and printing, and possibly distribution, of the Book. It is not necessary
that the District bear the entire cost of production and/or distribution, so long as its share
of the expenditure amounts to more than $50 in public funds. (Imperial Advice Letter,
No. A-98-103; Battersby Advice Letter, No. A-94-303.)

Notwithstanding a possibility that the District may eventually recover the costs
incurred in production and/or distribution of the Book through sales of copies retained by
the District, we must conclude that the District will have met the "public funds" threshold
of regulation 18901(a)(3) once it has spent more than $50 on production or distribution of
the Book.

4. Distribution of more than 200 copies in any calendar month.

The Act's mass mailing restrictions limit only distribution, by means specified in
regulation 18901(a)(1), of more than two hundred copies of "substantially similar items"
within a calendar month. We presume that every copy of the book will be "substantially
similar" to every other copy, and have concluded that the 200 copy limitation applies
regardless of whether the Books are distributed by the publisher, the District, or both in
combination. You indicate that the District intends to limit its own distribution of the
Book to fewer than 200 copies per month. But as explained above, to avoid violation of
the mass mailing restriction it would be necessary to ensure that the District and the
publisher together do not distribute, by means listed in regulation 18901(a)(1), more than
200 copies of the Book in any single calendar month.

4 You have told us that the photographs in which the officers will appear are "group" photographs.
However, the regulation does not contain an exception for "group" photographs which, indeed, may
enhance the image of a particular officer pictured in the company of others more famous or celebrated.

File No. I-03-233
Page No. 6

Subdivision (b) of regulation 18901 provides a number of exceptions to the mass
mailing restriction discussed above. On present information, it does not appear that any
of these exceptions would apply to the District's anticipated production and distribution
of the Book.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure
